Q1: I tried to spread my studying throughout the week, aiming for about three hours a day when I could manage it. Being a kinesthetic learner, I couldn't just sit and read for hours, so I'd break things up with practice problems or walking around while reviewing notes. Honestly, my routine was more about finding pockets of time between everything else going on rather than having a perfect schedule. I'd often study late at night when it was quiet and I could focus without distractions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, like always reviewing material on Sunday evenings, but when deadlines piled up, everything went out the window. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course because other classes were demanding more attention, then I'd have to catch up in intense bursts. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new assignments or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline coming up and that would push me to log in. I also logged in when I felt like I was falling behind and needed to see where I stood with the course materials. It wasn't really a daily habit for me, more like when something specific needed my attention.
Q4: There was a stretch in the middle of the semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every couple of days. It happened because I started connecting it to my morning routine before heading out. But honestly, that habit kind of faded when I got bored with the content and other things started competing for my attention. It never became as automatic as checking my phone or anything like that.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful because I could pause and rewind when something didn't click. I also found the practice quizzes helpful since I learn better by doing things rather than just reading. The discussion posts were okay too because seeing how other students explained concepts sometimes gave me a different angle on the material. Anything hands-on or interactive worked best for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary reading materials and the extra resource links. With limited time and honestly feeling pretty bored with a lot of the content, I had to prioritize what would actually help me pass. The lengthy PDF documents just weren't engaging enough for me to sit through when I could get the main points from videos. I figured if it wasn't going to be on an exam, it wasn't worth my limited study time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could actively do something rather than passively consume information, that made a huge difference. I also valued anything that gave me immediate feedback so I knew if I was understanding things correctly. When something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I lost interest fast.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford. With limited resources and time, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities got done because they affected my grade, but optional stuff always got pushed to the bottom of the list. It's not that I didn't want to learn more, but survival mode meant focusing on what counted officially.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also made physical flashcards and practiced problems in a notebook since writing things out helps me remember better. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me than the official course materials.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd print out practice problems and work through them at the library, or when I'd study with a classmate and we'd quiz each other verbally. I also spent time just thinking through concepts while doing other things, like during my commute. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual learning happening in my head.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice if it was complicated, then I'd have my notes and downloaded materials to keep reviewing. Internet access isn't always reliable for me, so downloading everything I could was just practical. I probably watched some lectures three or four times total, but the LMS would only show me accessing them once or twice.
Q12: The middle of the semester was rough when the content got more abstract and I couldn't connect it to anything practical. I also hit a wall when assignments started piling up across all my courses at the same time. The boredom really set in when I felt like I was just going through motions without understanding why any of it mattered. Those CBT sessions actually helped me push through some of that mental resistance.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with stuff at home, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other obligations that felt more urgent. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because logging in just reminded me how lost I felt. Fatigue made everything take longer, so my engagement looked lower even when I was trying.
Q14: There were times I'd have the lecture video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. Sometimes I'd log in, look at the dashboard, and just feel too mentally drained to actually engage with anything meaningful. The activity was there but the learning definitely wasn't happening in those moments.
Q15: I put off discussion posts more than anything because they felt tedious and I wasn't sure what to say. Some assignments got delayed because I was waiting until I felt like I understood the material well enough to attempt them. I knew procrastinating was hurting me, but sometimes the boredom made it really hard to start. The resilience I've built up usually kicked in eventually, but not always before I'd lost some points.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active in the days before. My login patterns probably looked like spikes around due dates rather than steady engagement. It's not the healthiest approach, but when you're juggling a lot with limited resources, deadlines create the urgency that gets things done.
Q17: When I got feedback that was actually helpful and specific, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But when feedback was delayed or generic, it didn't really change my behavior much. Good grades on assignments made me feel like my approach was working, so I'd keep doing what I was doing. Negative feedback sometimes made me avoid that type of activity for a while before I could face it again.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me know what to expect and plan around. Having clear modules made it easier to chunk my studying into manageable pieces. But when the course had too many small activities spread throughout, it felt overwhelming and I'd miss things. I appreciated when the important stuff was clearly marked so I could prioritize with my limited time.
Q19: The video content being available to download made engagement way easier for someone in my situation. Clear deadlines and organized modules helped me stay on track. What made it harder was when there were too many discussion requirements or activities that felt like participation for participation's sake. Also, some of the content just wasn't engaging enough to hold my attention when I was already feeling bored with the subject.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something they need but not finding it, so they keep clicking around. Others might be anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Some people might also be dealing with stuff outside of school that prevents them from finishing assignments even though they're trying to engage. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I performed reasonably well on exams and assignments despite not being super active in the LMS because I did a lot of my real studying offline. Some students are efficient and don't need to click through everything multiple times to learn it. Others, like me, use external resources and methods that don't show up in the system but still lead to learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are active and what they click on, which gives some insight into behavior. But they miss the quality of engagement and whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, mental effort, or understanding that happens away from the screen. They show activity, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm curious about something and want to figure it out, even if it's challenging. For me, engagement feels like being mentally present and actively working through problems, whether that shows up online or not. It's about the effort and intention behind the activity, not just the activity itself.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied using downloaded materials or handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with classmates working through problems together. It also can't show my mental state or how much resilience it took to keep pushing through when I was bored and had limited resources. My real engagement story is way more complex than any login data could capture.
